speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Linda Behr. The date is March 1, 1997. I'm interviewing Lyonell Fliss in Johannesburg, South Africa. Take five.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife at the same design office where I was working in Bucharest. She was a drafts woman there where I was an engineer, but different sections. It was a huge share government company, about 1,500 people working for industrial projects of the steel industry. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Her name was Lillianna Weiner of Jewish Transylvanian origin. Her parents were speaking Hungarian in their house and of course Romanian. We married in 1966. And since we married, we had in mind that one day we may be so lucky to be able to emigrate from Romania. The opportunity came in 1968, 69, when the turmoil in Eastern Europe happened with the occupation of Czechoslovakia. Her father was a manager of large hotels in Bucharest and at the sea resorts. He was speaking a number of languages. He was a very interesting. He's still alive in Bucharest, Franz Weiner. He had the a life that many people would be envious to have stories like this to tell. During the war he was involved in an espionage network for the allies. And he escaped from Romania during the war, went to Istanbul, and held there a pub where all the spies of various sides were meeting. It was a meeting place for spies from England and America and from Germany and Italy and Japan were coming in his pub. And actually his story is partially told in a movie, which was shown on the television. It was called And his pub was also shown there. His name, Franz Weiner. He was decorated after the war by the Marshall Alexander of the British forces. But this was his bad luck, because when the communists took power, considered him that he was a British spy and put him straight into jail, not considering that he was a anti-Nazi fighter. This was their interpretation. And he suffered many years from the harsh treatment in jail. But when he came back from the jail, through his very good background in the hotel industry and in the restaurant industry, he proved that he is a valuable asset for the Romanian tourist industry. So he was put in charge of large hotels and restaurants. Through this type of activity he managed to get contacts with people who were dealing with issuing passports in Romania. So probably through bribing or other reasons, he managed in the year of 1969-- so a year after the occupation of Czechoslovakia by the Soviet army-- managed to obtain for Lillianna and I passports to visit Czechoslovakia. This wasn't considered a very big deal normally, because it was still inside the Iron Curtain. It was from one jail to another jail. But in terms of Jews who applied for immigration from Romania and who were considered enemies of the regime, Lillianna and I couldn't get even this sort of passports. So through her father we managed to obtain these passports. And now we heard various stories about people who managed from Czechoslovakia which had a common border with Austria, which was considered the Western world. People managed to escape. So in our minds immediately we made the plan. And we managed to save and buy a small motorcar in Romania after many years of waiting in a queue for buying such a commodity. And with the passport, which was valid from Romania to Czechoslovakia via Hungary and back, to be returned, and through a certain defined route, which needed to be watched, we prepared ourselves. We filled the car with food and all what was necessary for a trip of two weeks. And without telling anybody about our intention of escaping-- particularly we didn't tell our parents because they would have been in a state to know that we risk our lives or our freedom. The minimum sentence for such an attempt to escape was five years in jail, or, if you tried to do this in a more adventurous way, trying to break through or to go through the Iron Curtain, you can be shot dead. But we planned our escape. We arrived safely to Hungary from Romania. And it was a possibility that we can escape from Hungary to Yugoslavia. Arriving through the Hungarian Yugoslavian border my wife, who spoke Hungarian through her parents, asked the Hungarian guards if there is a possibility to try to visit somebody in Yugoslavia, not telling them that we intend to go. And she was lucky to find somebody who understood what we intend to do to advise her not even to dream to try such a thing, because the border is sealed. So it was second time after the first time in Iasi when my mother saved my life with knowledge of a foreign language, that a foreign language looked to be very useful, sometimes saving your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went on our route to Czechoslovakia. We arrived at Bratislava And there is the border over the Danube River to Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very hesitant to go straight to the border. I wanted to stay, to spend a couple of days in Bratislava to see what the situation is. It was a state of uncertainty at that time. But sometimes I came to the conclusion that women, they have a sixth sense. And Lillianna said we have to try now. Let's try right now instead of spending time around.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we headed towards the Iron Curtain zone with our little motorcar. The Iron Curtain actually is not an Iron Curtain in the shape of a wall. It's a full zone about 2 or 3 kilometers wide, full of land mines. It used to be-- now it doesn't exist the Iron Curtain. Full of barbed wire and all kind of military installations.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a preliminary border posted. The entrance into this zone where you enter, nobody asks you anything. But it's a one-way trap. Once you enter this zone, you can't get out without their permission. It's like a mousetrap.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went through. Nobody said anything. But you can't get out. After a couple of kilometers, the road stops to the real border post of the Czechoslovakian Austrian border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now there were two possibilities, that the guards were either Russians or Czechs. If they were Russians, they would have stamped out passports with their stamp, sent us back, and when you have to return the passport in Romania, you have this additional stamp, which sends you straight to jail. Or the other possibility, they will be Czech, Czechoslovakian border guards who may be more understanding.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we took a 50-50 chance. We went there. And to our relief, there was Czechs guarding the border at that particular time. Now we weren't speaking Czech. They didn't speak Romanian. And we didn't have any common language to discuss the matter. So we took our passports, which were valid for Czechoslovakia only. We had no business to be there. And we handed the passports to the officer. He took the passports into their gate house. And it took about 10 minutes until he came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now I can tell you that 10 minutes, it's a very long time. For me, for us, it was like 10 years. We were counting seconds. He came out, didn't say a word. He gave us the passports back. And asked us to open the [INAUDIBLE] to see what's in. He opened the [INAUDIBLE]. He had a look. I was shivering. My wife was pale. We didn't know what happens.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we entered into the car, and we didn't know what to do. He didn't say a word. So we started slowly moving the car towards the barrier. It was 50 meters away was the barrier from the Austrian side.

speaker: interviewee
text: The guard at the barrier opened the barrier. And we slowly moved under the barrier, just moved about 10 meters beyond the barrier. We were in no man's land. And we heard a shout from the back, halt. Now I had two options, either to accelerate the car. But I was sure that a bullet is faster than a car. Or to stop and come back, and we were so close to freedom and so far from the freedom.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided to leave the car stationary in that position and come back by foot, and with my wife in the car. My legs were jelly. I walked this 10 meters. It was like a marathon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guard showed me that the lid of the [INAUDIBLE] was open. I don't know how I crawled back to the car. And I started moving so slowly. The Austrian side opened the barrier. And I didn't recall anything. I fainted on the other side. My wife was stronger than me. She was still able to stand. So then we were there. And straight from there we asked where the Israeli embassy is in Vienna. We drove to Vienna after I recovered from the shock. And it was simple from there. Almost simple, not that simple-- we stayed for two weeks in Vienna. And one night we decided to go to the Prater. Prater is the entertainment park of Vienna. We parked the car which had a Romanian number plate. We went to see a movie. When we came we so a character standing next to our car and inspecting our number. It was like a character from 'Thriller' in a black leather raincoat asking us what we are doing there with a Romanian car in Romanian. Immediately we realized that he was a member of the security services on the Romanian government, and the role of these people was to catch people who escape and to murder them. Normally there were a number of occasions where there were Bulgarian members of the security service with umbrellas with poison needles injecting people who they wanted to get rid of them, or simply taking them to a dark place and shooting them. It was like being there and not being there. Eventually we managed to get rid of him. It was a place where there were lots of people and through the crowd managed to escape. From there we went to Israel where we spent six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you to get to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [non-English speech] organized our travel to Israel. We had with us one suitcase.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been be able to contact your family in Bucharest?

speaker: interviewee
text: We sent a cable from Vienna to Bucharest that we arrived safely. They didn't know. They saw that we are on holiday in the Romanian mountains. And they couldn't understand what's all this. Eventually they understood. Because we didn't tell them a word.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they found out from this cable that we are not in the mountains. We are-- we were scared that they will suffer from our defection. But actually nothing happened to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my parents-- this was in October, November 1969-- in April of 1970 my parents also emigrated to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they manage to emigrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were old. They have pensions. They had a flat. The flat had to be donated to the government. The pensions were cancelled. All my father's lifetime was taken away. They were already old enough for them to get rid of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your life in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel I worked as an engineer in a number of places. Of course I spent the Yom Kippur War on the front line, on the [non-English speech]. After the Yom Kippur War I couldn't find employment. The economic situation was very poor. Particularly the building industry was affected. And I tried to get a place where I can practice my profession. And the place was South Africa, where I found employment with one of the major civil engineering companies, [INAUDIBLE] [? Roberts. ?] And since then I still am with this company. I'm now their chief civil engineer. And I managed quite well. I had regrets that I had to leave Israel. But on the other hand everybody's life is organized by himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your reception in Israel? Did you feel welcome?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mixed. I felt welcomed by friends that I knew. But by the locals and by the [INAUDIBLE] and by the people established there a long time ago, the reception was very cold. I was disappointed. I came with very patriotic feelings and ideas. I was disappointed, for instance, that in the army, I wasn't considered for any single [INAUDIBLE] in spite of being trained in engineers corps, which is one of the dangerous military professions, dealing with mines and landmines and other installations. And I was used only as a guard at a kibbutz. I don't say that it's not important to be  a guard there, but I saw that they didn't give me the right recognition when I arrived in Israel. I was also quite unhappy in my work there. I couldn't get satisfaction in my work and what I was doing. I had in the past in Romania was involved in large projects, which were a professional challenge for me. And I was always a challenger. And I found that this possibilities of large, industrial projects for which I learned so many years, and I practiced, and I dedicated my best years, I find here in South Africa better opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your life in South Africa.

speaker: interviewee
text: My life I would say was a happy life here all these years with the exception of some tragic events. 10 years after we arrived here my wife, Lillianna, who was a tower of strength and who was an addict bodybuilder, going to gym five times a week, and all of a sudden she developed lung cancer without any sort of signs, warning signs before. We had just returned from holiday in Europe, happy return, nothing happened to her. And all of a sudden she developed this lung cancer, and in six months time she was dead. I was very, very depressed for a long time. Then meeting through friends Anita, I remarried after three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel in South Africa? Was it more like home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Actually I managed to get familiar with the South African lifestyle. I have to admit that the local population, Jewish population and not so Jewish population, and particular Jewish population, didn't welcome me so much when I arrived here. And it took a long time to create ties, friendly ties with people. But this didn't bother me too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you for being back to Bucharest or Iasi?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time I returned to Iasi was when I was living in Bucharest after graduation after each the 1958 application for immigration to Israel when I was sent to Iasi in way of punishment. So I came back to Iasi for the first time after we left during the war as sort of a punished person.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel going back there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was difficult. I avoided to go in the area where we were marched to death. I couldn't do this. I lived in a totally different area, and I was busy day and night on the construction site. And I was doing the things which didn't bring me the memories of the place. I survived. I think that I didn't feel too much about what the past brought to me.
